title: Restriction Map in Cohomology
speaker: Hélène Esnault
abstract:
Motivated by the instances of Grothendieck’s generalized Hodge conjecture which have a purely algebraic expression, we study the restriction map in cohomology from a smooth projective variety X
 to an affine U. Starting from X being defined over an affine S smooth over ℤ, we consider de Rham cohomology over a dense set of closed points in S. Then the restriction is ‘controlled’ by the global differential forms on X. When we go p-adically we prove the ’same’ result with values in a certain separated quotient of Bhatt-Scholze prismatic cohomology which maps to the separated quotient of derived p-complete de Rham cohomology. 
We construct examples which show that one needs an extra assumption to lift this control to the whole cohomology.
Caro-D’Addezio showed that for derived p-complete de Rham cohomology modulo p-torsion it is controlled by H1 of differential forms in addition. We lift this to prismatic cohomology modulo I-torsion, which also has a corollary on étale cohomology of the rigid analytic fibre.

proof_stages.label: Grothendieck restriction problem and algebraic de Rham reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates Grothendieck's generalized Hodge-coniveau restriction conjecture and isolates the special case H^0(X,Ω^i)=0, where torsion and comparison theorems reduce the desired vanishing on an affine open to an algebraic de Rham question after spreading out over arithmetic models.

proof_stages.label: Test cases and failure of naive open-size criteria
proof_stages.summary: Known low-degree cases and geometric test examples show that the open set must be chosen carefully and that neither being affine nor Faltings-small is by itself a sufficient explanation of the predicted restriction vanishing.

proof_stages.label: Mod-p equivalence via Cartier and conjugate spectral sequence
proof_stages.summary: For good reductions, the speaker proves a characteristic-p criterion: absence of global i-forms is equivalent, on a dense set of reductions, to vanishing of the de Rham restriction map for every affine open, using base change, affineness, the conjugate spectral sequence, Cartier isomorphism, and the converse via semicontinuity/Serre-type input.

proof_stages.label: p-complete de Rham and prismatic topological framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up prismatic cohomology over a prism (A,I), the D_n=d·φ(d)···φ^{n-1}(d) topology, its specialization to p-complete de Rham cohomology, separated quotients, nonseparated closures of zero, and the interpretation of kernels as strongly p- or D_n-divisible algebraic classes.

proof_stages.label: Vanishing in separated prismatic cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The main prismatic theorem, attributed jointly with Mark and Sasha, says that under the no-i-forms hypothesis the restriction from proper X to any affine U vanishes after passing to the separated quotient of prismatic cohomology, with the de Rham version following by specialization, while explicit computations show that full prismatic cohomology still retains some algebraic classes.

proof_stages.label: Nonseparated kernel and multiplicative obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Further de Rham results of Marco and Daniel identify additional vanishing modulo p-torsion under an H^1-type hypothesis, and a cup-product argument shows that classes lying in the nonseparated/continuous part have products killed p-adically, so p-completed cohomology loses essential ring information from the original algebraic problem.

proof_stages.label: Prismatic lift, rigid consequence, and role of D_n
proof_stages.summary: The final theorem lifts the Daniel-Marco de Rham statement to prismatic cohomology and, via comparison, to the rigid analytic/étale setting, while the Q&A explains that the D_n system rather than naive d^n-powers is crucial because Frobenius moves the successive prism lines and keeps the needed directions separated.

examples.name: Classical Hodge conjecture as a middle-degree case
examples.note: The speaker notes that Grothendieck's generalized Hodge-coniveau statement contains the usual Hodge conjecture when the relevant sub-Hodge structure is in middle degree, illustrating the strength of the opening problem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes Grothendieck’s general statement by taking the middle-degree case i even and c=i/2 to explain how the classical Hodge conjecture is recovered

passes.reason: The case c=i/2 is the maximal Hodge-coniveau boundary case, used only to show the generalized conjecture contains the classical Hodge conjecture.
The classical Hodge-conjecture specialization is the level-zero Hodge-class case at maximal c=i/2, used only to show containment.
The classical Hodge case is the level-zero Tate, type-(p,p) specialization at maximal coniveau, cited as a contained case rather than a failure.
It is the specialization c=i/2, the maximal coniveau middle-type case inside the generalized Hodge conjecture, used to show containment rather than failure.
The classical Hodge conjecture is invoked as a standard middle-type specialization of Grothendieck’s broader conjecture, not as a structurally exceptional object or a failure case.
the classical Hodge conjecture appears as the boundary case c=i/2 of the generalized Hodge-coniveau setup, used only as an inclusion example
The classical Hodge conjecture is used as a contained special case, with c=i/2 the maximal middle Hodge-class/coniveau case.
It is the maximal-coniveau middle case c=i/2 recovering ordinary Hodge classes, used as a contained special case rather than a failure.
Used only to show Grothendieck’s conjecture contains the classical Hodge conjecture, with the (p,p) case at maximal coniveau.
It is invoked as a contained special case, with c=i/2 the maximal-coniveau middle Hodge-class case.

examples.name: Product of two varieties with no forms
examples.note: For X=Y×Z with H^0(Y,Ω^a)=H^0(Z,Ω^b)=0, the product open is presented as a concrete family where the restriction question can be formulated algebraically and later supplies product classes in the prismatic discussion.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sets X=Y×Z with no a- and b-forms, identifies the product cohomology class H^a(Y)H^b(Z) inside H^{a+b}(X), and discusses the resulting restriction-vanishing/product-open behavior

passes.reason: A standard product situation with vanishing-form hypotheses, used as a positive test case rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Known degree-one and degree-two cases
examples.note: The cases i=1 and i=2 illustrate what is already understood: i=1 is algebraic via hard Lefschetz, while i=2 uses the exponential sequence and GAGA to relate H^{1,1} to Néron-Severi classes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the i=1 and i=2 cases, deriving vanishing from absence of forms, Hard Lefschetz, the exponential sequence, GAGA, and Néron–Severi generators

passes.reason: The i=1 and i=2 cases are low-degree base cases where the Hodge-theoretic problem collapses to familiar algebraic facts.

examples.name: P^1×P^1 minus the diagonal
examples.note: This large affine open first defeats the naive guess that affine size or Faltings-smallness controls the restriction map, and later reveals the good/bad news that separated prismatic cohomology kills too much while the full prismatic class can survive.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes U=P^1×P^1 minus the diagonal, checks affineness via the A^1-fibration, computes why CH^2 does not die, discusses Faltings-smallness, and later tracks a specific class in prismatic cohomology

passes.reason: P^1×P^1 minus the diagonal is a highly symmetric very large affine open, used to show such a large affine open still need not kill the relevant class.
P^1×P^1\Δ is a highly regular large affine diagonal complement, used to show that such a large affine open need not kill the relevant CH2/cohomology class.

The homogeneous open surface \(\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^1\setminus\Delta\) is a maximally large affine open in this setting, and it is used to show that such a large affine open need not kill the relevant Chow/cohomology classes.

examples.name: Enriques-surface warning for Faltings-small opens
examples.note: The cited Enriques-type surface examples have Faltings-small affine opens whose restriction kernel is only one-dimensional, serving as a counterexample to the idea that smallness alone solves Grothendieck's restriction problem.
examples.engagement_reason: the Enriques-surface case is cited as a warning by stating the existence of Faltings-small opens with a one-dimensional kernel, without constructing or verifying the example in the talk

passes.reason: Enriques surfaces form a standard family, and the example is used to warn that even Faltings-small affine opens may leave nonzero cohomology image.
